editableNewDialogue.text: Hey—quick question. What’s the best way to figure out a song when you only remember one vague lyric?

A good approach is to narrow by (1) approximate year, (2) genre/tempo, (3) vocalist type, and (4) a few “sounds like” phrases. Then try: lyric search with quotes + wildcards (e.g., "never * you go"), search by misheard lyrics (“mondgreen”), and check platforms that index audio (Shazam/SoundHound, or YouTube’s search suggestions). If it might be from a game/movie, soundtrack lists help too.
Does searching with quotes actually help, or is it basically the same as normal search?

Quotes help when the phrase is exact; without quotes, results can be loosely related. If the lyric might be slightly off, do both: one quoted search and one unquoted search with extra context words (genre, year, “lyrics”, “remix”, “radio edit”). You can also try substituting similar words: “never let you go”, “never gonna let you go”, “I won’t let you go”, “don’t let go”.
Any tricks for when the lyric is probably misheard?

Yes: try phonetic spellings (how it sounded), search fragments (2–4 words), and add the vibe: “EDM”, “uplifting”, “female vocal”, “2010”. Also check lyric sites that allow partial matches. If you can hum it, audio search is often faster than text.

Unrelated example: a lot of people misheard “Hold me closer, tiny dancer” as “Tony Danza,” and searching the misheard version still often surfaces the correct song.
If I only remember the melody, is there a tool that works reliably?

Humming tools can work: Google’s “Search a song” (mobile) is decent, and Shazam can sometimes identify hummed or played melodies if the pattern is clear. They’re less reliable for very generic chord progressions.

Another unrelated tidbit: the most common pop progression is often described as I–V–vi–IV, which is why some melodies feel familiar across many tracks.
What info should I write down before I forget it?

Write: (1) any exact words you’re confident about, (2) a rough BPM/tempo (“fast like a club track”), (3) voice type (female/male, solo/duet), (4) instruments/synth style, (5) where you heard it (radio, playlist, game), (6) year range, and (7) any similar songs it resembles.

Also, for tracking leads, it helps to keep a short list of “ruled out” candidates so you don’t loop back later.
If it helps at all, I’m usually hunting these tracks on my phone while I’m on the metro, so I can’t really play long previews—just quick chorus snippets.
Got it—short chorus snippets only. If you tell me which app you’re using (YouTube, Spotify, SoundCloud, etc.), I can tailor the search approach to fast checks and timestamped chorus sections.
Hey, quick correction about that old song hunt: I wasn’t saying I *prefer* lesser-known EDM pop from 2009–2014 in general. That was just me narrowing down clues for one specific track.
Got it—so those details were constraints for that particular mystery song, not your overall taste.

Exactly. I listen to all kinds of stuff. The only stable thing is: for *that* song, I was sure it had female vocals and an EDM-ish feel, and I thought I heard it around 2009–2014. The “<1M views” thing was just a guess for that one track.
Thanks for clarifying. I’ll treat “female vocals + EDM-ish + 2009–2014 + maybe not popular” as temporary search criteria for that one song, not as a long-term preference.
